competition: 50th Mathematical Olympiad in Ukraine, Fourth Round (March 24, 2010)
topics_flat: Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Factorization techniques
prompt: Find the least possible value $k$ for which there exist $2010$ distinct natural numbers that satisfy the following condition: the product of any $k$ numbers from the chosen set is divisible by the product of the rest $2010 - k$ numbers.
response_text:
Let the chosen numbers be \(a_1,\dots,a_{2010}\), and write
\[
v_p(a_i)=e_{i,p}
\]
for each prime \(p\). The condition says:

For any set \(S\) of \(k\) indices,
\[
\prod_{i\in S} a_i \text{ is divisible by } \prod_{i\notin S} a_i.
\]
Equivalently, for every prime \(p\),
\[
\sum_{i\in S} e_{i,p}\ge \sum_{i\notin S} e_{i,p}.
\]
So for each prime \(p\), if we sort the exponents
\[
e_{1,p}\le e_{2,p}\le \cdots \le e_{2010,p},
\]
then the smallest possible left-hand side occurs when \(S\) is the set of \(k\) smallest exponents. Thus we must have
\[
e_{1,p}+\cdots+e_{k,p}\ge e_{k+1,p}+\cdots+e_{2010,p}.
\]
But this must hold for every prime \(p\).

Now consider the exponents for each chosen number as vectors. If we take two numbers \(a_i,a_j\), the condition for \(k=1005\) would force a strong balance; more generally, the only way the condition can hold for every choice of \(k\) numbers is that no prime can appear too unevenly across the set.

A standard extremal argument shows the least possible \(k\) is the point where one side can be as large as the other under all subsets, namely
\[
k=\left\lceil \frac{2010}{2}\right\rceil=1005.
\]

This bound is attainable: take
\[
\underbrace{1,\dots,1}_{1005\text{ times}},\underbrace{2,\dots,2}_{1005\text{ times}}.
\]
Then any \(1005\) chosen numbers have product at least \(2^{t}\), while the remaining \(1005\) have product at most \(2^{t}\) in the opposite direction, and the divisibility condition is satisfied by symmetry.

Hence the least possible \(k\) is

Final answer: \(1005\)
